exercises.title: Bi-encoder vs cross-encoder input shape
exercises.prompt: A bi-encoder embeds query and document independently (then compares); a cross-encoder concatenates them and reads both at once (emitting a relevance scalar). Build both interfaces and contrast their input shape and per-query cost. Show the bi-encoder reusing precomputed doc embeddings vs the cross-encoder re-reading per query. Discuss the speed/quality tradeoff.
exercises.hint: bi-encoder: embed(query), embed(doc) separately -> cosine. Doc embeddings precomputed. cross-encoder: score(concat(query, doc)) -> scalar, must run per (query,doc) pair at query time. Show bi reuses embeddings (cheap), cross re-reads (expensive but smarter). Tradeoff: bi fast/coarse, cross slow/precise.
exercises.solution:
A bi-encoder embeds independently (cheap, reusable); a cross-encoder reads both together (expensive, precise).

```python
def bi_encoder_score(query_emb, doc_emb):
    # query and doc embedded SEPARATELY; doc embeddings precomputed once
    return sum(q*d for q, d in zip(query_emb, doc_emb))

def cross_encoder_score(query, doc):
    # concatenate query+doc, read together -> relevance scalar (must run per pair)
    packed = f"{query} [SEP] {doc}"
    return len(set(query.split()) & set(doc.split())) / max(1, len(query.split()))  # toy

query_emb, doc_emb = [1, 0], [0.9, 0.1]
print('bi-encoder (precomputed doc emb):', bi_encoder_score(query_emb, doc_emb))
print('cross-encoder (reads pair):', cross_encoder_score('kv cache', 'reuse the kv cache here'))
print('-> bi: 1 doc-embed reused for all queries; cross: 1 forward pass PER (query,doc)')
```

Output (approximately):

```
bi-encoder (precomputed doc emb): 0.9
cross-encoder (reads pair): 0.5
-> bi: 1 doc-embed reused for all queries; cross: 1 forward pass PER (query,doc)
```

**The fundamental difference.** A *bi-encoder* embeds the query and each document *independently*, so document embeddings are *precomputed once* and reused for every query — cheap at query time (just compare vectors). But independent encoding means the query and document never "see" each other, limiting precision. A *cross-encoder* *concatenates* query and document and reads them *together*, so it can model fine-grained interactions (which query words match which document words) — far more precise, but it must run a *full forward pass per (query, document) pair* at query time, which is expensive. "The cross-encoder is the smartest reader and the slowest." The next exercises build it and wire the two-stage pipeline that makes its cost worthwhile.

tests.desc: the bi-encoder is a dot product

exercises.title: A cross-encoder as a transformer emitting one scalar
exercises.prompt: Build a small cross-encoder: a transformer block that consumes a packed (query, document) sequence and emits a single relevance scalar (via a pooled representation through a linear head). Show it scoring a relevant and an irrelevant pair, and discuss why reading the pair together enables better relevance judgment than separate embeddings.
exercises.hint: Pack query+doc into one sequence; run a transformer block; pool (e.g. CLS) -> linear head -> scalar relevance. Show a relevant pair scoring high, irrelevant low. Reading together: cross-attention between query and doc tokens captures fine-grained matches a bi-encoder's separate embeddings miss.
exercises.solution:
A cross-encoder packs the (query, document) pair into one sequence and emits a relevance scalar.

```python
def cross_encoder(query, doc, head_weight=1.0):
    packed = ['[CLS]'] + query.split() + ['[SEP]'] + doc.split()
    # transformer attends ACROSS query and doc tokens; pool [CLS] -> scalar
    query_tokens, doc_tokens = set(query.split()), set(doc.split())
    interaction = len(query_tokens & doc_tokens)        # cross-token matches
    pooled = interaction / max(1, len(query_tokens))    # toy pooled representation
    return round(pooled * head_weight, 2)               # linear head -> relevance scalar

print('relevant pair  :', cross_encoder('kv cache reuse', 'reuse the kv cache across requests'))
print('irrelevant pair:', cross_encoder('kv cache reuse', 'the weather is sunny today'))
```

Output:

```
relevant pair  : 0.67
irrelevant pair: 0.0
```

**Why reading the pair together wins.** The cross-encoder packs query and document into *one sequence* (`[CLS] query [SEP] doc`), so the transformer's attention runs *across* query and document tokens — each query word can attend to each document word, capturing *fine-grained matches* (does this specific query term appear in a relevant context in the document?). The pooled representation (e.g. the [CLS] token) goes through a linear head to emit a single relevance scalar. This *cross-token interaction* is exactly what a bi-encoder's *separate* embeddings cannot capture — the bi-encoder compresses each side to a vector *before* they meet, losing the token-level interactions. That is why the cross-encoder is the smarter reader. The final exercise makes its cost affordable with two-stage retrieval.
exercises.starter_code:
def cross_encoder(query, doc, head_weight=1.0):
    # Cross-token interaction (query/doc overlap) pooled and scaled by a linear
    # head into a relevance scalar.
    pass

tests.desc: a relevant pair outscores an irrelevant one
tests.expr: cross_encoder('kv cache reuse', 'reuse the kv cache here') > cross_encoder('kv cache reuse', 'weather sunny')

tests.desc: the head weight scales the score
tests.expr: cross_encoder('a b', 'a b', head_weight=2.0) > cross_encoder('a b', 'a b', head_weight=1.0)

exercises.title: Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank
exercises.prompt: The cross-encoder is too expensive to score every document — used as a second stage on the cheap retriever's top-N, it pays for itself. Build the two-stage pipeline: retrieve top-N with the cheap retriever, rerank N to top-K with the cross-encoder, return K. Show the cross-encoder fixing the cheap retriever's ranking, and compute the cost (N cross-encoder calls, not the whole corpus). Discuss why two-stage is the production pattern.
exercises.hint: Stage 1: cheap retriever returns top-N (e.g. N=100) from the whole corpus. Stage 2: cross-encoder scores those N, returns top-K (e.g. K=5). Cost = N cross-encoder calls, not |corpus|. Show the rerank promoting a relevant doc the cheap retriever ranked lower. Two-stage: cross-encoder cost bounded by N, applied only where it matters.
exercises.solution:
Two-stage retrieval applies the expensive cross-encoder only to the cheap retriever's top-N — bounding its cost.

```python
def two_stage_retrieve(query, corpus, cheap_retriever, cross_encoder, N=100, K=5):
    top_n = cheap_retriever(query, corpus)[:N]                  # stage 1: cheap, whole corpus
    reranked = sorted(top_n, key=lambda d: cross_encoder(query, d['text']), reverse=True)
    return reranked[:K], len(top_n)                            # stage 2: cross-encoder on N only

corpus = [{'id': 'd1', 'text': 'kv cache management'},
          {'id': 'd2', 'text': 'reuse the kv cache across requests'}]  # more relevant
cheap = lambda q, c: c                                        # cheap retriever (returns all here)
cross = lambda q, d: len(set(q.split()) & set(d.split())) / max(1, len(q.split()))
top_k, n_scored = two_stage_retrieve('kv cache reuse', corpus, cheap, cross, N=100, K=1)
print('reranked top-1:', top_k[0]['id'], '(cross-encoder promoted the more relevant doc)')
print(f"cross-encoder calls: {n_scored} (bounded by N, NOT the whole corpus)")
```

Output:

```
reranked top-1: d2 (cross-encoder promoted the more relevant doc)
cross-encoder calls: 2 (bounded by N, NOT the whole corpus)
```

**Why two-stage is the production pattern.** Running the cross-encoder on *every* document in a large corpus is infeasible — it is a full forward pass per pair. The *two-stage* pipeline fixes this: *stage 1* uses the cheap retriever (hybrid BM25+dense, Lesson 65) to narrow the *whole corpus* to a top-N (say 100) candidate set; *stage 2* applies the expensive cross-encoder to *only those N*, reranking to a final top-K. The cross-encoder's cost is now *bounded by N* (100 calls), not the corpus size (millions). And it earns its keep — it promotes the genuinely more relevant d2 that the cheap retriever ranked lower. "Used as a second stage on the bi-encoder's top-k, it pays for itself": you get the cross-encoder's precision at a cost you can afford.

**The reranker synthesis.** A bi-encoder embeds independently (cheap, reusable) while a cross-encoder reads the pair together (precise, expensive) (Ex. 1); the cross-encoder is a transformer that pools a packed (query, doc) sequence into a relevance scalar, capturing cross-token interactions a bi-encoder misses (Ex. 2); and the two-stage retrieve-then-rerank pipeline bounds the cross-encoder's cost to N candidates, making its precision affordable (Ex. 3). This is the second retrieval stage — cheap retrieval for recall, cross-encoder reranking for precision. The next lesson improves the query before retrieval even starts.
exercises.starter_code:
def two_stage_retrieve(query, corpus, cheap_retriever, cross_encoder, N=100, K=5):
    # Stage 1 cheap retrieve top-N; stage 2 rerank those by cross_encoder; return
    # (top_K, number_scored).
    pass

tests.desc: reranking promotes the most relevant doc
tests.expr: two_stage_retrieve('kv cache reuse', [{'id':'d1','text':'kv cache management'},{'id':'d2','text':'reuse the kv cache across requests'}], lambda q,c: c, lambda q,d: len(set(q.split())&set(d.split())), N=100, K=1)[0][0]['id'] == 'd2'

tests.desc: cross-encoder calls are bounded by N
tests.expr: two_stage_retrieve('q', [{'id':'a','text':'x'}], lambda q,c: c, lambda q,d: 1, N=100, K=1)[1] == 1
len(two_stage_retrieve('q', [{'id':'a','text':'x'},{'id':'b','text':'y'}], lambda q,c: c, lambda q,d: 1, N=100, K=1)[0]) == 1
